required.title: [Read Aloud / Look Together] Where the Wild Things Are
required.author: Maurice Sendak

suggested.title: [Look Together] Any great picture book with strong visual storytelling

competence_markers.description: The student can follow a musical narrative (like "Peter and the Wolf") and describe what the music represents — characters, events, and moods
The student can identify narrative elements in picture book illustrations and explain how pictures carry story meaning
The student has created a simple sequential visual story (three to six images) that communicates a narrative without words
The student demonstrates the beginning of architectural awareness — noticing and describing how different buildings and spaces feel and identifying design features that create those feelings
The student can compare how music and pictures each tell stories, identifying at least one similarity and one difference
The student can articulate what they observe in a space beyond "I like it" — using descriptive vocabulary about size, light, materials, and mood
The student engages with narration prompts with specific detail and personal interpretation

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me the story of "Peter and the Wolf" using what you heard in the music.
narration_prompts.detail: Which instruments played which characters? What happened? How did the music tell you what was happening?

narration_prompts.prompt: Pick a picture book we studied and tell me how the pictures tell the story.
narration_prompts.detail: What do the pictures show you that the words do not? How do the pictures change from beginning to end?

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about the story you drew.
narration_prompts.detail: What happens? How did you show what was happening without using any words?

narration_prompts.prompt: Describe two different buildings we visited.
narration_prompts.detail: How did they feel different? What made one feel different from the other? What do you think the person who designed each building wanted you to feel?

narration_prompts.prompt: How can music tell a story without words?
narration_prompts.detail: What tools does the composer use — instruments, loud and soft, fast and slow — to make you understand what is happening?

narration_prompts.prompt: What is the most interesting building or room you have ever been in?
narration_prompts.detail: What made it special? How did it make you feel? What about the design created that feeling?

cross_connections.description: The rhythmic sense you built in 3.4.1 helps you follow the pacing and energy of program music. A fast rhythm means something different from a slow rhythm — and now you hear that difference as part of a story.
The structural listening skills from 3.4.2 — hearing melody, dynamics, tempo — are exactly the tools you use to follow a musical narrative.
The careful observation skills from 3.4.4 are what allow you to really see what picture book illustrators are doing. The drawing skills let you create your own visual stories.
Telling stories in pictures and in music uses the same narrative structures — beginning, middle, end — that you develop when you write. Narrating what you experienced builds the same skills.

The ancient civilizations you studied built extraordinary buildings — pyramids, ziggurats, temples. Now you are beginning to understand why those buildings looked the way they did and how they made people feel.

Sacred buildings — churches, mosques, temples — are designed to express what the people who built them believed about God, the world, and their place in it. Reading buildings is part of reading cultures.

content.why_this_matters:
People have always told stories. They told stories around fires, painted stories on cave walls, carved stories into stone, and eventually sang stories in music and built stories into buildings. Stories are not just something you read in books. Stories live in paintings, in songs, in the rooms you walk through, and in the buildings you see every day.

In this packet, you will learn that music can tell a story — not with words, but with sound. You will learn that pictures in a book are not just decoration — they carry the story forward, sometimes more powerfully than the words. You will begin to draw your own stories, one picture at a time. And you will start to look at buildings — really look at them — and notice that a church feels different from a grocery store, that a library feels different from a gym, and that someone designed those spaces to make you feel a certain way.

This is where the things you have been learning about music and the things you have been learning about art come together. Both music and art tell stories. Both use patterns, rhythm, and structure to carry you through an experience. When you learn to see stories in sound and stories in pictures, you begin to understand something powerful: **the whole world around you has been designed, and you can learn to read it.**
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Musical storytelling awareness** — the ability to hear a story in music without words
2. **Visual narrative literacy** — understanding how pictures tell stories, especially in picture books
3. **Sequential drawing** — creating a simple story through a series of images
4. **Architectural awareness** — the first habit of noticing buildings and spaces as designed environments
5. **Cross-medium perception** — the beginning ability to see that stories can be told in sound, image, and built space
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Music That Tells Stories

You already know that music can make you feel things — happy, sad, excited, calm. But music can also tell you a story. Not with words (though sometimes words are part of it), but with sound itself.

When a composer writes music that tells a story, we call it **program music**. The music follows a program — a plan, a story, a sequence of events. The composer uses instruments, melodies, rhythms, and dynamics to represent characters, actions, and places.

**How music tells stories:**
- **Different instruments represent different characters.** In "Peter and the Wolf," the bird is a flute because flutes are light and quick. The grandfather is a bassoon because bassoons are low and grumbly. The wolf is French horns because they sound powerful and dangerous.
- **Melodies can be like names.** Each character gets their own melody — their own musical name. When you hear that melody, you know that character is part of the scene. This is called a **theme** or a **leitmotif** (a fancy word for "a melody that stands for something").
- **Loud and soft tell you what is happening.** Quiet music might mean something is sneaking up. Loud, crashing music might mean something dramatic or dangerous is happening. Getting louder (crescendo) builds excitement. Getting softer (decrescendo) releases it.
- **Fast and slow set the pace.** A chase scene needs fast music. A peaceful moment needs slow music. The tempo of the music is like the speed of the story.

When you listen to program music, your job is to follow the story with your ears. What is happening now? Who is here? Is the mood changing? What do you think will happen next?

### Part 2: Pictures That Tell Stories

Every time you look at a picture book, you are reading two stories at once — the story in the words and the story in the pictures. Sometimes they tell the same story. Sometimes the pictures tell you things the words do not say.

**How pictures tell stories:**
- **Size matters.** In *Where the Wild Things Are*, the pictures start small, surrounded by white space. As Max's adventure grows, the pictures grow too — getting bigger and bigger until they burst across two full pages with no words at all. Then, as the adventure ends and Max comes home, the pictures shrink back down. The size of the picture IS the size of the adventure.
- **Color sets the mood.** Dark colors can mean danger, sadness, or mystery. Bright colors can mean joy, energy, or safety. When the colors change in a picture book, the mood of the story is changing too.
- **What the character is doing tells you how they feel.** Is the character's body hunched over or standing tall? Are their eyes wide or narrow? Are they moving toward something or away from it? Illustrators use body language just like actors do.
- **Sequence creates time.** When you see three pictures in a row — a door closed, a door opening, a person walking through — you experience time passing. Each picture is a moment. The space between pictures is where your imagination fills in what happened.

The most powerful proof that pictures can tell stories is a **wordless picture book** like *The Arrival*. There are no words at all. Every single part of the story — the emotions, the events, the meaning — is carried entirely by images. If you can follow a wordless picture book, you can read visual narrative.

### Part 3: Drawing Your Own Stories

Now it is your turn. You are going to tell a story using only pictures — a series of drawings, in order, that show something happening from beginning to end.

This is called **sequential art** — art that happens in a sequence, one image after another, to create a story. Comic books and graphic novels use sequential art. So do storyboards for movies. So did ancient Egyptians, who painted sequences of images on tomb walls to tell stories about the afterlife.

**How to draw a story:**
- **Start simple.** Three pictures: a beginning, a middle, and an end. That is all you need for a story. A bird sits on a branch. A cat appears below. The bird flies away. Three pictures, one story.
- **Show, do not tell.** You are not writing words — you are drawing. If your character is scared, draw them looking scared. If something is far away, draw it small. If something is important, draw it big.
- **Keep the same character recognizable.** If your character wears a red hat in the first picture, they should wear a red hat in every picture. The viewer needs to know who they are following.
- **Think about what happens between the pictures.** The space between your drawings is where the viewer's imagination works. You do not need to draw every single moment — just the moments that matter most.

### Part 4: Looking at Buildings

You spend most of your life inside buildings. But have you ever really looked at one? Not just used it — walked into a store, sat in a classroom, eaten in a kitchen — but actually looked at it the way you look at a painting or listen to a piece of music?

Buildings are designed. Someone decided how tall the ceiling would be, where the windows would go, what material the walls would be made of, how the light would enter, and how the space would feel. Those decisions affect you every time you walk into a room — even if you never notice.

**Start noticing:**
- **How does this room feel?** Some rooms feel cozy and warm. Some feel cold and empty. Some feel grand and important. Some feel cramped and uncomfortable. The feeling is not an accident — it comes from the design.
- **Where does the light come from?** Is the room bright with sunlight from big windows? Is it dim with small windows? Is it lit by harsh overhead lights? Light changes everything about how a space feels.
- **What is it made of?** Stone walls feel different from wooden walls. Tile floors feel different from carpeted floors. Brick feels different from glass. The materials a building is made of communicate something — warmth or coldness, heaviness or lightness, permanence or temporary.
- **Why does a church look different from a grocery store?** Because they serve different purposes, and the people who designed them wanted you to feel different things. A church often has high ceilings to make you look up, colorful windows to fill the space with colored light, and heavy materials to make you feel the weight of something lasting. A grocery store has low ceilings, bright fluorescent lights, and wide aisles — designed for efficiency, not wonder. Neither is wrong, but they are very different, and the difference is intentional.

The habit of noticing buildings and spaces is the beginning of **architectural literacy** — the ability to read the built world the way you read a book or listen to a piece of music.
content.formative_practices:
### Study 1: The Peter and the Wolf Listening [Early in the Packet]
Listen to Prokofiev's "Peter and the Wolf" together — the full piece with narration. Before listening, learn which instrument represents which character. During listening, follow the story through the music. After listening, the student narrates: What happened in the story? Which character's music did you like best? Can you hum their theme?

### Study 2: Picture Book Investigation [Early-to-Mid Packet]
Choose three picture books with strong visual storytelling. For each one, go through the pictures slowly — looking at each illustration carefully before reading the text. What is the picture telling you that the words are not? How do the pictures change from beginning to end? What does the illustrator do to show you how a character feels?

### Study 3: The Wordless Story [Mid-Packet]
Study *The Arrival* (or another wordless picture book) together. The student follows the story through the pictures alone and narrates what is happening. Key question: How did the artist tell you the whole story without a single word? What did the pictures do that words might not have been able to do?

### Study 4: Drawing a Story [Mid-to-Late Packet]
The student creates a sequential story in three to six drawings. The subject is their choice — something that happens, with a beginning, middle, and end. No words allowed. After finishing, show the drawings to someone who has not seen them. Can that person follow the story? If so, the visual narrative works.

### Study 5: The Carnival of the Animals Game [Ongoing]
Listen to movements from "Carnival of the Animals." For each one, the student draws the animal they hear in the music — before being told which animal it is. Then compare: did the music paint the right picture in your mind? This exercise builds the connection between auditory and visual imagination.

### Study 6: Building Walk [Late Packet]
Visit at least three different kinds of buildings together — a house of worship, a library or public building, and a store or commercial space. At each one, pause and notice: How does this space feel? Why? What about the ceiling, the light, the materials, the size? What is different about this building compared to the last one? The student sketches at least one building and describes how it made them feel.

### Study 7: Musical Storytelling Creation [End of Packet]
The student creates their own simple program music — using percussion instruments, a keyboard, or even their voice — that tells a very short story (a storm approaching and passing, an animal waking up and going on an adventure, etc.). They perform it and then explain what each part of the music represents.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Musical Narrative Comprehension
**Developing well:** The student follows program music with engagement, can identify which instruments represent which characters, and narrates the musical story with detail. They anticipate what might happen next based on what the music is doing.

**Developing slowly:** The student hears music as pleasant or unpleasant background but cannot track a narrative through it. Characters and their themes do not register.

**Diagnostic questions:** Was the story introduced clearly before listening? Was the piece too long for the student's attention span? Try shorter excerpts before attempting full works.

### 2. Visual Narrative Literacy
**Developing well:** The student can "read" picture book illustrations for narrative content beyond what the text states. They notice changes in picture size, color, and composition. They can follow a wordless picture book.

**Developing slowly:** The student sees pictures as decoration for the text, not as narrative carriers. They do not notice visual changes across the book.

**Diagnostic questions:** Have you slowed down enough? Are you asking specific questions about individual illustrations, or rushing through the whole book?

### 3. Sequential Art Creation
**Developing well:** The student creates a multi-image sequence that communicates a story. Someone unfamiliar with the student's intentions can follow the narrative.

**Developing slowly:** The student draws unrelated images or a single scene rather than a sequence. There is no narrative progression.

**Diagnostic questions:** Has the student planned the story verbally before drawing? Breaking it into "first... then... finally..." before picking up a pencil often unlocks the sequential thinking.

### 4. Architectural Awareness
**Developing well:** The student spontaneously comments on spaces — noticing how rooms feel, comparing buildings, attending to light and materials. They use descriptive vocabulary naturally.

**Developing slowly:** The student does not notice their physical environment as a designed space. Buildings are just "places you go."

**Diagnostic questions:** Have you modeled the noticing? Walk into a space and say aloud: "This ceiling is very high — it makes me feel small. Look at all the light coming in from those windows." The student learns the habit by watching you practice it.
mentor_guide.common_pitfalls:
### Treating This as "Music Class" Plus "Art Class"
3.4.6 is specifically about the convergence. If you do a listening activity one day and a drawing activity the next with no connection between them, the student misses the point. Keep asking the bridging questions: "The music told a story with sound. The picture book told a story with images. What is similar about how they did it?"

### Skipping the Architecture Component
Architecture is the easiest element to skip because it requires leaving the house or at least looking at your environment with new eyes. Do not skip it. Architectural literacy is a lifelong skill, and this is when the habit begins.

### Over-Explaining Program Music
You do not need to give a lecture on Prokofiev before pressing play. Introduce the characters and their instruments, listen, and then talk about what happened. The music does the teaching. Your job is to prepare the student to hear it and then to draw the narration out afterward.

### Prioritizing Drawing Quality Over Narrative
The sequential art exercise is about storytelling, not draftsmanship. If the student is anxious about whether their drawings are "good enough," redirect: "Can someone else follow your story? Then the drawings work."
mentor_guide.narration_evaluation:
### Prompt 1: Peter and the Wolf
**Strong:** Retells the story with reference to specific instruments and how the music communicated the narrative — the bird's flute theme, the wolf's horns, the suspense of the hunt.
**Weak:** "It was about a wolf." No connection between music and story.

### Prompt 2: Picture Book Visuals
**Strong:** Identifies specific visual storytelling techniques — changes in picture size, color shifts, character expressions — and explains how they carry the narrative.
**Weak:** Summarizes the text of the book without discussing the pictures.

### Prompt 3: Their Own Story
**Strong:** Explains the narrative clearly, describes their visual choices ("I made the storm clouds dark because it was scary"), and can tell whether others understood the story.
**Weak:** Cannot explain what their drawings are about, or the sequence has no narrative logic.

### Prompt 4: Buildings
**Strong:** Describes specific qualities of different spaces — light, height, materials, mood — and makes comparisons. Offers observations about why spaces feel the way they do.
**Weak:** "The church was nice." No specific observations.
